tasks.title: Exercise a real CLI iteration against a temp workspace and tighten any artifact or verifier gaps it exposes

tasks.title: Align nested inspection-root and execution-root semantics across prompting, execution, and verification

tasks.notes: CLI execution now passes the selected execution root into `codex exec --cd`, and validation/git verifiers run from the selected verification root instead of depending on manual `cd ... && ...` command composition.

tasks.title: Add an explicit inspection-root override for ambiguous umbrella or multi-repo workspaces

tasks.title: Multi-agent readiness: task ownership, concurrent reconciliation, and remediation isolation

tasks.title: Add atomic task-claim mechanism so agents cannot concurrently select the same task
tasks.notes: tasks.json is a flat file with a single-writer assumption. Two agents selecting the same task produce last-write-wins corruption that T23 drift detection catches only after damage is done. The fix is an atomic claim step: an agent writes a claim record before execution begins, the control plane validates the claim is still current before reconciling the completion report, and stale or contested claims are surfaced in preflight.

tasks.notes: Added RalphTaskClaimStatus, RalphTaskClaim, and RalphTaskClaimFile to src/ralph/types.ts for a flat, inspectable task-claim schema consistent with tasks.json.

tasks.title: Remove operator workspace state from the committed repository
tasks.notes: EXECUTE BEFORE T24. state.json (6265 lines of machine-specific runtime paths), done-task-audit-current-worktree.md, and logs/extension.log are committed to the repo, meaning every clone starts with broken path references pointing at /home/admin/Documents/repos/Ralph and a misleading preloaded iteration history. The .ralph/.gitignore already excludes /artifacts, /logs, /prompts, and /runs but misses state.json and ad-hoc audit files. This is the highest-priority fix because it unblocks contributors and corrects the credibility contradiction of a durable-state tool shipping a broken instance of its own durable state.

tasks.title: Replace the permanent multi-agent deferral with an explicit acceptance-criteria milestone
tasks.notes: EXECUTE BEFORE T24. The docs validator currently enforces that docs/boundaries.md contains the fragment 'single-agent iteration/loop runner', which means the validator is a mechanism for locking in indecision rather than tracking a milestone. There is no acceptance criterion anywhere that would cause someone to say the deferral is lifted. T24 is the work that satisfies those criteria, so the criteria must be written before T24 runs — otherwise T24 has no done definition. Fixing this first also means T26.3 can validate that T24's tasks.json entries map to the documented criteria.

tasks.notes: EXECUTE BEFORE T24. iterationEngine.ts is 2050 lines and owns completion-report parsing, JSON extraction, reconciliation write logic, task decomposition proposal building, provenance bundle persistence, artifact cleanup, preflight coordination, loop orchestration, and the two public entry points. tasks.title: Add a continuous automated ledger-integrity gate to npm run validate
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T24. The done-task audit that caught T21's tracker drift was a manually-run one-off. Ralph shipped with a done parent and all-todo children — the exact condition T23 was written to detect — and no automated gate caught it before commit. T23 added preflight blocking at runtime, but preflight only runs when Ralph executes inside VS Code. A check:ledger script that validates tasks.json at npm run validate time would have caught T21 before it was committed. Placing this after T24 means the ledger check can also validate claim records once T24.1's claims.json schema exists.

tasks.title: Add scripts/check-ledger.js that validates tasks.json for drift, cycles, and orphaned parents
tasks.notes: Added a standalone scripts/check-ledger.js with drift, missing-reference, dependency-cycle, and optional claims validation plus a focused regression test file. The task validation command passes on the current workspace.

tasks.title: Wire check:ledger into package.json scripts and npm run validate
tasks.notes: Add a check:ledger script to package.json: 'node scripts/check-ledger.js'. Update the validate script to run check:ledger after check:docs and before the test suite so a drifted ledger blocks the full validation gate before running tests. Update docs/testing.md under Authoritative Commands to list npm run check:ledger and describe what it validates. Add the required fragment 'check:ledger' to the DOC_RULES entry for docs/testing.md in src/validation/docsValidator.ts.
tasks.validation: cd ralph-codex-vscode-starter && npm run check:ledger && npm run check:docs

tasks.title: Add regression tests for the ledger check script covering each drift class
tasks.notes: Add test/checkLedger.test.ts with test fixtures covering: a clean tasks.json exits 0; a done parent with a todo child exits 1 with the parent's id in the output; a missing dependsOn target exits 1; a missing parentId target exits 1; a dependency cycle exits 1; a valid in_progress task with a matching claims.json entry exits 0; a valid in_progress task with no claims.json present exits 0 (backward-compatible). Use child_process.spawnSync to invoke the script directly so the test covers the real exit-code contract rather than a mocked internal. Run npm run validate to confirm all gates pass.

tasks.title: Surface the trust-chain epistemic gap explicitly in the provenance bundle and documentation
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T24. The provenance bundle is presented as the flagship audit artifact, but it proves only that a specific string of text was handed to a black box. The completion-report.json artifact already captures the model's self-report, and reconciliationWarnings on RalphIterationResult already captures divergence between that self-report and what the verifier found — but neither is surfaced in the provenance bundle itself, and the documentation does not label the epistemic gap explicitly. In a multi-agent deployment this gap is more consequential: an audit trail that does not distinguish model claims from verifier evidence is harder to use for compliance purposes when multiple agents are producing interleaved artifacts.

tasks.title: Add completion-report divergence fields to RalphProvenanceBundle and persist them in the bundle artifact
tasks.notes: Added completion-report divergence fields to RalphProvenanceBundle, persisted them from executed iteration results including completion-report.json when present, added Model Self-Report summary output, and covered the rejected-report reconciliation case with regression tests.

tasks.title: Add an Epistemic Gap section to docs/provenance.md documenting what the bundle cannot prove
tasks.notes: Added Epistemic Gap section to docs/provenance.md covering the five required points: CLI provenance boundary, completionReportStatus machine-readable label, reconciliationWarnings semantics, absence-of-warnings meaning, and operator guidance to treat verifier artifacts as authoritative.

tasks.title: Add docsValidator fragment enforcement for the epistemic gap language
tasks.notes: Add requiredFragments to the DOC_RULES entry for docs/provenance.md in src/validation/docsValidator.ts: 'model self-report', 'unverified', 'reconciliationWarnings', and 'authoritative evidence'. Add 'Epistemic Gap' to the requiredHeadings list for docs/provenance.md. This ensures the epistemic gap documentation cannot be silently removed during future edits without breaking the validation gate. Run npm run validate to confirm.

tasks.title: Document the prompt budget methodology and make policies model-configurable
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T24. Every token target in the prompt budget policy matrix was derived empirically against Codex CLI with no documented methodology. If Codex CLI changes its context behaviour, or if Ralph is used with a different model (which T24 makes structurally possible), every budget number becomes wrong in ways that are silent — prompts will be trimmed according to a stale policy and the evidence records will show within budget against a target that was never re-validated. This task documents the derivation methodology, adds a calibration helper, and introduces a model-configurable budget profile so different adapters can carry different policies.

tasks.title: Create docs/prompt-calibration.md documenting how token targets were derived and how to re-derive them
tasks.notes: Create ralph-codex-vscode-starter/docs/prompt-calibration.md. It must document: (1) the target model and context window size that the current policy matrix was calibrated against; (2) the methodology used to arrive at each token target — specifically whether targets were set by observing real truncation behaviour, by estimating from section sizes, or by a different method; (3) a step-by-step recalibration procedure an operator could follow when switching to a different model or after a Codex CLI context-window change; (4) the mapping between ralphCodex.reasoningEffort values and their expected token overhead so operators can account for reasoning tokens when setting targets. Add the doc to REQUIRED_DOCS, REQUIRED_AGENTS_DOC_MAP_TARGETS, and REQUIRED_README_DOC_MAP_TARGETS in docsValidator.ts, and add a link in AGENTS.md and README.md.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.promptBudgetProfile setting and wire it into promptBuilder's policy selection
tasks.notes: Add a ralphCodex.promptBudgetProfile string setting to package.json contributes.configuration with a default value of 'codex' and an enum of ['codex', 'claude', 'custom']. In src/config/types.ts add promptBudgetProfile to the config type. In src/prompt/promptBuilder.ts, make the policy matrix selection branch on promptBudgetProfile: 'codex' uses the current hardcoded targets; 'claude' uses a separate policy matrix with targets appropriate for Claude's longer context window (document the placeholder targets in docs/prompt-calibration.md as requiring calibration before production use); 'custom' reads token targets from a ralphCodex.customPromptBudget object setting. Add regression coverage in test/promptBuilder.test.ts for the claude profile selecting a different policy matrix than the codex profile.

tasks.title: Add a budget calibration script that renders prompts across all kinds and reports estimated token counts
tasks.notes: Create ralph-codex-vscode-starter/scripts/calibrate-prompt-budget.js. The script should accept a workspace path argument, load a minimal tasks.json and prd.md from that workspace, render one prompt of each kind (bootstrap, iteration, fix-failure, continue-progress, replenish-backlog, human-review-handoff) for both cliExec and ideHandoff targets using the existing promptBuilder logic, and print a table showing: prompt kind, target, estimated token count, current budget target, and delta. The script is not part of npm run validate — it is a manual operator tool for recalibration. Document its usage in docs/prompt-calibration.md under the recalibration procedure. Add it to docs/testing.md under What Is Not Covered as a manual calibration tool.

tasks.title: Add prompt quality tests that assert on rendered content, not just section inclusion policy
tasks.notes: EXECUTE LAST. The 801-line test/promptBuilder.test.ts verifies that sections are included or omitted according to the budget policy — zero assertions exist about whether the rendered text would cause a model to do the right thing. This is the most consequential untested output Ralph produces. This task is placed last because it benefits from: (1) the clean module boundaries from T27 making promptBuilder.ts easier to test in isolation; (2) the model-configurable budget profiles from T30 providing test dimensions; (3) T24's agentId fields giving the prompt fixture framework an additional variable to cover. Golden snapshot tests are the pragmatic first step — they do not prove prompt quality but they make regressions visible and force authors to consciously update snapshots when prompt content changes.

tasks.title: Build a prompt test fixture framework with representative workspace and task scenarios
tasks.notes: Create test/fixtures/promptScenarios.ts exporting a set of at least five named scenario objects, each containing: a minimal RalphTaskFile, a prd.md string, a progress.md string, a WorkspaceScan snapshot, and a prior iteration result (or null for bootstrap). The scenarios should cover: (1) a fresh workspace with no prior iterations; (2) a workspace mid-task with a prior partial_progress iteration; (3) a workspace with a repeated_no_progress stop and a pending decompose_task remediation; (4) a workspace with a blocked task; (5) a fix-failure scenario with a non-null validationFailureSignature. These fixtures are the inputs that T31.2 and T31.3 render prompts against. Keep each fixture minimal — the goal is controlled, reproducible inputs, not realistic project content.

tasks.title: Add golden snapshot tests for each prompt kind rendered against the fixture scenarios
tasks.notes: Add test/promptBuilder.snapshot.test.ts. For each combination of prompt kind and fixture scenario that is valid (e.g. fix-failure only against scenarios with a prior failed iteration), render the prompt using the existing promptBuilder logic and compare the output against a stored snapshot in test/fixtures/snapshots/. Use Jest's toMatchSnapshot or a custom snapshot mechanism that writes plain .md files so snapshot diffs are readable in code review. The first run should write the snapshots. Subsequent runs detect regressions. Add a note to docs/testing.md that snapshot updates require running npm test -- --updateSnapshot and that reviewers should read snapshot diffs as carefully as code diffs.

tasks.title: Add content assertion tests for critical prompt sections across key scenarios
tasks.notes: Add content assertions to test/promptBuilder.test.ts (or a new test/promptBuilder.content.test.ts) that go beyond section-presence checks to assert on rendered text. At minimum: (1) a fix-failure cliExec prompt rendered against the repeated-failure fixture must contain the task's validation command string in the rendered text, not just assert that the execution section is included; (2) a human-review-handoff prompt rendered against the blocked fixture must contain the blocker text from the task; (3) a replenish-backlog prompt must contain the PRD objective text; (4) an iteration prompt rendered against the remediation fixture must contain a substring from the remediation summary; (5) a bootstrap prompt must not contain any prior-iteration context. These assertions are brittle by design — they should fail if the template rendering logic accidentally drops critical content under budget pressure.

tasks.title: Auto-restart the extension host on control_plane_reload_required instead of waiting for operator action
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T27 (iterationEngine decomposition), BEFORE T33. control_plane_reload_required is not an error — the iteration completed cleanly before the stop. The only reason a human is involved is that Ralph has no mechanism to trigger an extension host restart itself. VS Code exposes vscode.commands.executeCommand('workbench.action.reloadWindow') which restarts the extension host without closing the window. In loop mode this stop reason should auto-trigger that command after persisting the iteration result, rather than surfacing it as an operator decision point. This is the lowest-risk human-removal change in the codebase because the state is already clean when the reload fires.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange boolean setting defaulting to false
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange to package.json contributes.configuration as a boolean with default false and a description stating that when enabled, Ralph will automatically execute workbench.action.reloadWindow after a control_plane_reload_required stop in loop mode instead of waiting for operator action. Add the corresponding field to the config type in src/config/types.ts and the read path in src/config/readConfig.ts. Default is false so existing behaviour is unchanged until the operator explicitly opts in.

tasks.title: Wire auto-reload into the loop command handler when the stop reason is control_plane_reload_required
tasks.notes: In src/commands/registerCommands.ts, in the Run CLI Loop command handler, after an iteration returns with stopReason === 'control_plane_reload_required': if autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange is true, log a message to the output channel stating Ralph is reloading the extension host to apply control-plane changes, wait 1500ms to ensure the iteration result artifact has been flushed to disk, then call vscode.commands.executeCommand('workbench.action.reloadWindow'). The wait is intentional — do not skip it. If autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange is false, preserve the existing behaviour of surfacing the stop to the operator. Do not trigger auto-reload from the Run CLI Iteration single-shot command — only from the loop path.

tasks.title: Add regression coverage and document the auto-reload behaviour in docs/workflows.md
tasks.notes: Add tests in test/commandShell.smoke.test.ts covering: auto-reload is not triggered when autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange is false; auto-reload is not triggered from the single-shot iteration command even when the setting is true; the reload command is invoked with the correct VS Code command id when the setting is true and the stop reason is control_plane_reload_required. Mock vscode.commands.executeCommand in the test environment. Add a Control-Plane Reload subsection to the Run The Ralph Loop section of docs/workflows.md explaining the setting, the 1500ms flush delay, and that reloadWindow is only safe here because the iteration result was already persisted before the reload fires. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Auto-apply decompose_task and mark_blocked remediation actions without operator intervention
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T28 (ledger gate) and T24.1 (task claiming). The manual Apply Latest Task Decomposition Proposal command exists because writing to tasks.json felt consequential at the time. But buildRemediationArtifact already validates proposals at construction: child tasks are one level deep, capped at three, inherit only the parent validation command, and are rejected at write time if they create duplicates or graph invalidity. The human in this loop is not adding judgment — they are running a command that Ralph could run itself. decompose_task auto-application is safe under those hard constraints. mark_blocked is safer still — it is additive, setting a status and blocker field without restructuring the graph. reframe_task is explicitly excluded from auto-application because it changes the semantic meaning of a task title and scope, which requires human intent. request_human_review is a hard stop by definition and must never be auto-applied.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.autoApplyRemediation setting with decompose_task and mark_blocked as opt-in actions
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.autoApplyRemediation to package.json contributes.configuration as an array of strings with items enum ['decompose_task', 'mark_blocked'] and default empty array. This means auto-application is fully opt-in — an operator who wants both actions automated sets ['decompose_task', 'mark_blocked']; an operator who only wants mark_blocked sets ['mark_blocked']; the default empty array preserves current behaviour exactly. Do not include reframe_task or request_human_review in the enum — they must not be auto-applicable. Add the field to src/config/types.ts and src/config/readConfig.ts.

tasks.title: Implement auto-application of mark_blocked in the iteration engine after a blocked stop
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/iterationEngine.ts (or src/ralph/reconciliation.ts after T27.3), after the iteration result is classified and persisted with stopReason === 'repeated_identical_failure' and remediation.action === 'mark_blocked': if 'mark_blocked' is in autoApplyRemediation, acquire the task file lock via withTaskFileLock (T24.2.1), read the current tasks.json, set the selected task status to 'blocked' and populate the blocker field with remediation.summary, write the file, release the lock, and log to the output channel that mark_blocked was auto-applied with the task id and blocker text. Record the auto-application in the iteration result warnings array with the text 'Remediation auto-applied: mark_blocked on task <id>' so the provenance bundle reflects what happened. Run npm test.

tasks.title: Implement auto-application of decompose_task using the existing apply-proposal write path
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/iterationEngine.ts, after the iteration result is classified and persisted with remediation.action === 'decompose_task' and suggestedChildTasks is non-empty: if 'decompose_task' is in autoApplyRemediation, call the same validated write function used by the Apply Latest Task Decomposition Proposal command — do not duplicate the write logic. The existing write path already: validates the parent task exists and is not done, rejects duplicate child ids, rejects graph-invalid proposed dependencies, and appends children atomically. Wrap the call in withTaskFileLock. Record the auto-application in the iteration result warnings array with 'Remediation auto-applied: decompose_task on task <id>, added <n> child tasks'. Run npm test.

tasks.title: Add regression coverage for auto-remediation and document the behaviour in docs/verifier.md and docs/workflows.md
tasks.notes: Add tests in test/iterationEngine.integration.test.ts covering: mark_blocked is auto-applied when the action matches and the setting includes it; decompose_task is auto-applied and the resulting tasks.json contains the expected child tasks; neither action is applied when the setting is empty; reframe_task is never auto-applied regardless of setting; request_human_review is never auto-applied regardless of setting; auto-application is recorded in the iteration result warnings. In docs/verifier.md under Task Remediation, add a paragraph describing the autoApplyRemediation setting, which actions are eligible, and why reframe_task and request_human_review are excluded. Add a brief note to docs/workflows.md under Run The Ralph Loop. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Automatically continue into replenish-backlog prompt when backlog is cleanly exhausted in loop mode
tasks.notes: EXECUTE AFTER T28 (ledger gate). When the durable backlog is cleanly exhausted Ralph already selects the replenish-backlog prompt kind — but the loop currently stops with no_actionable_task before executing it, requiring the operator to manually rerun. If the ledger is consistent (no drift detected by T28's check-ledger gate) and the stop reason is no_actionable_task with backlog.actionableTaskAvailable === false, the loop can safely continue into the replenishment iteration automatically. The replenishment prompt is deterministically generated and the resulting tasks.json still gets implicit human review when the operator decides whether to continue the next loop. The ledger gate from T28 is the safety dependency — auto-continuing into replenishment is only safe when the ledger is provably consistent, not when the exhaustion is a false positive caused by drift.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.autoReplenishBacklog boolean setting and wire it into the loop stop decision
tasks.notes: Added ralphCodex.autoReplenishBacklog config plumbing and updated loop stop logic to continue into backlog replenishment only when no actionable task remains, backlog.actionableTaskAvailable is false, and preflight shows no ledger-drift safety errors. Added loop logic coverage for the enabled path and the ledger-drift gate.

tasks.title: Add regression coverage and document auto-replenishment in docs/workflows.md
tasks.notes: Add tests in test/loopLogic.test.ts covering: auto-replenishment continues the loop when the backlog is clean and the setting is true; auto-replenishment does not continue when ledger drift is present in preflight diagnostics even if the setting is true; auto-replenishment does not continue when autoReplenishBacklog is false; the loop still stops when no_actionable_task is accompanied by an error-severity ledger-drift diagnostic regardless of the setting. In docs/workflows.md under Run The Ralph Loop, add a Backlog Replenishment subsection explaining that auto-replenishment is safe only when the ledger is consistent, why the drift check is the safety gate, and how to use the setting. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Add a fully-autonomous loop mode that composes T32, T33, and T34 with explicit hard stops preserved
tasks.notes: EXECUTE LAST, after T32, T33, and T34 are all complete. T32 (auto-reload), T33 (auto-remediation), and T34 (auto-replenishment) each introduce an individual opt-in setting. An operator who wants maximum autonomy should not need to set three separate flags. A ralphCodex.autonomyMode: 'supervised' | 'autonomous' setting that implicitly enables all three when set to 'autonomous' is cleaner to operate and makes the intent explicit. Critically, autonomous mode must document and enforce the hard stops that remain: needs_human_review and request_human_review are non-negotiable halts; the initial PRD must be human-authored; autonomous mode does not bypass preflight blocking. The hard stops are not configurable.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.autonomyMode setting and wire it as a shorthand for the three individual autonomy settings
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.autonomyMode to package.json contributes.configuration as a string enum with values 'supervised' (default) and 'autonomous'. In src/config/readConfig.ts, after reading individual settings, apply the override: if autonomyMode === 'autonomous', set autoReloadOnControlPlaneChange to true, autoApplyRemediation to ['decompose_task', 'mark_blocked'], and autoReplenishBacklog to true, regardless of their individual values. If autonomyMode === 'supervised', individual settings are used as-is, allowing mixed configurations. Log the effective autonomy configuration to the output channel at extension activation so the operator can confirm what mode is active. Add the field to src/config/types.ts.

tasks.title: Document autonomous mode, its hard stops, and the trust model in docs/boundaries.md and docs/workflows.md
tasks.notes: Add an Autonomous Loop Mode section to docs/workflows.md covering: what autonomyMode: autonomous enables; the hard stops that are never automated (needs_human_review, request_human_review, initial PRD authorship); the safety dependencies (T28 ledger gate must be passing, T24 claim ownership must be active for multi-agent use); a recommended pre-flight checklist before enabling autonomous mode (clean ledger, human-reviewed tasks.json, validation command confirmed executable). Add a brief paragraph to docs/boundaries.md under Control-Plane Boundary noting that autonomous mode does not change the principal-agent model — the operator remains the principal and the hard stops are enforced regardless of mode. Add 'autonomyMode' and 'hard stops' as required fragments to the DOC_RULES entry for docs/boundaries.md in docsValidator.ts. Add regression coverage in test/commandShell.smoke.test.ts confirming that needs_human_review stops the loop even when autonomyMode is autonomous. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Md under Control-Plane Boundary, replace the sentence 'Broad multi-agent orchestration stays deferred until nested workspace, inspection, execution, and verification root policy remains deterministic, test-backed, and evidence-backed
tasks.notes: Updated the Control-Plane Boundary wording to describe the shipped state as a sequential single-agent iteration/loop runner and kept the docs validator fixture aligned with that wording.

tasks.title: The fragment 'single-agent iteration/loop runner' must still appear in the file to keep the existing docsValidator rule passing — keep it in the sentence describing the current shipped state
tasks.notes: Updated the Control-Plane Boundary sentence to describe the current shipped state while retaining the required 'single-agent iteration/loop runner' fragment, and docs validation passed.

tasks.title: Fix concurrent-agent state.json collision — iteration numbers and artifact paths collide under concurrent starts
tasks.notes: EXECUTE FIRST, before any other todo work. When two or more Ralph loop instances start concurrently they both read the same nextIteration value from state.json before either writes it back, generating identical iteration numbers, artifact directories, and prompt paths — last writer wins and earlier agents' artifacts are silently overwritten. Observed in the first multi-agent runs: claims.json shows three run-i057 provenanceIds acquired by separate agents within 20 seconds, but only one iteration-057 artifact directory survives. state.json mutations have no lock analogous to withTaskFileLock on tasks.json. This task adds a withStateLock mechanism and makes nextIteration allocation atomic so each concurrent agent always receives a unique, collision-free iteration number before it generates any artifact paths.

tasks.title: Add withStateLock to stateManager.ts — a state.lock file mechanism wrapping every saveState call
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/stateManager.ts, add a withStateLock function modelled on withTaskFileLock in src/ralph/taskFile.ts: acquire an exclusive wx-open on state.lock in the same directory as state.json; retry up to lockRetryCount times with lockRetryDelayMs delay; return a lock_timeout result if the lock cannot be acquired; always release in a finally block. Wrap every saveState call site in stateManager.ts inside withStateLock so no state.json mutation can proceed without holding the lock. The lock is not held across the entire iteration — only for the duration of each read-modify-write cycle. Update docs/invariants.md to document the state.lock contract alongside the existing tasks.lock section.

tasks.title: Atomic nextIteration allocation — acquire the state lock and increment nextIteration before any artifact paths are generated
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/iterationPreparation.ts the iteration number is currently read from snapshot.state.nextIteration — a snapshot captured before any lock is held, so two concurrent agents can read the same value. Change the flow so the very first step of prepareIterationContext (or a new allocateIteration helper it calls) acquires the state lock, reads the live state.json, increments nextIteration, writes it back, and releases the lock — returning the freshly allocated number. All artifact path construction must use this allocated number, not the pre-lock snapshot value. The allocation write is intentionally minimal (only nextIteration changes) so the lock is held for the shortest possible time.

tasks.title: Regression coverage and invariant docs for concurrent state.json writes
tasks.notes: Add tests in test/stateManager.test.ts covering: concurrent saveState calls produce unique nextIteration values with no collision; withStateLock returns lock_timeout when the lock cannot be acquired within the retry window; the lock file is cleaned up when saveState throws. Add an integration test in test/iterationEngine.integration.test.ts proving two concurrent runCliIteration calls against the same workspace generate different iteration numbers and non-overlapping artifact directories. Update docs/invariants.md under a new State File Write Serialisation section documenting the state.lock contract, the minimal-hold-time rule, and that abrupt process termination leaves a stale state.lock requiring manual operator removal.

tasks.title: Named agent identities — ralphCodex.agentId setting so each loop instance carries a distinct identity through claims and artifacts
tasks.notes: All multi-agent runs so far use agentId: 'default' for every loop instance. This means claims.json cannot distinguish which physical agent holds a task, Show Status cannot report which agent is active on what, and the remediation isolation that scopes history by agentId is always comparing the same value. A ralphCodex.agentId setting lets each VS Code window or CLI invocation carry a human-readable name (e.g. 'builder-1', 'builder-2', 'reviewer') that flows through claims, iteration results, and status output — making multi-agent operations observable and the claim graph interpretable without reading raw provenanceIds.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.agentId string setting and thread it through claims, iteration results, and run records
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.agentId to package.json contributes.configuration as a string with default 'default' and a description noting it should be unique per concurrent loop instance. Add it to src/config/types.ts and src/config/readConfig.ts. In src/ralph/iterationEngine.ts replace the hardcoded DEFAULT_RALPH_AGENT_ID with the configured value when creating claims via acquireClaim and when building RalphRunRecord and RalphIterationResult — the agentId field already exists on both types, this task wires the configured value through. Add a preflight warning when agentId is 'default' and another active claim with agentId 'default' already exists, since that indicates two loops sharing the same identity. Also write a durable identity record to .ralph/agents/<agentId>.json on first use, accumulating: agentId, firstSeenAt, completedTaskIds (append each time a task is reconciled done by this agent), and touchedFiles (union of changedFiles from diff summaries for this agent's iterations). This record is append-only and never cleared by cleanup — it is the start of a per-agent work history that will eventually support capability-based task routing.

tasks.title: Surface per-agent active-claim state in Show Status and preflight output
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/statusReport.ts and src/ralph/preflight.ts update the claim-state summary to group active claims by agentId and show: agent name, claimed task id and title, claimed-at timestamp, and whether the claim is stale. When multiple agents are active this makes it immediately visible which agent owns what without inspecting claims.json directly. Add a regression test in test/statusReport.test.ts proving the per-agent grouping renders correctly for a two-agent scenario. Add a Multi-Agent Team section to docs/workflows.md with a subsection describing the agentId setting and the multi-agent status view.

tasks.title: Review agent role — a dedicated loop mode that audits code quality and proposes backlog tasks rather than making changes
tasks.notes: Build agents make code changes. A review agent does the opposite: reads the codebase, runs validation, identifies quality gaps or missing coverage, and emits structured findings as proposed backlog tasks — without modifying any source files. This creates a natural check on build-agent output: after a build agent completes a task, a review agent can independently verify the work and propose follow-up tasks if gaps remain. The review agent uses the same claims and completion-report machinery as build agents but its prompt instructs it to propose rather than implement, and its reconciliation path should surface any source-file modifications as an anomaly.

tasks.title: Add RalphAgentRole type and ralphCodex.agentRole setting; add review-agent prompt template
tasks.notes: Add RalphAgentRole = 'build' | 'review' | 'watchdog' | 'scm' to src/ralph/types.ts. Add ralphCodex.agentRole to package.json contributes.configuration as a string enum with default 'build'. In src/prompt/promptBuilder.ts branch on agentRole: when 'review', select a review-agent prompt template that instructs the model to: (1) run the validation command and report results; (2) inspect changed files since the last completed task; (3) identify missing test coverage, doc gaps, or invariant violations; (4) emit proposed follow-up tasks in the completion report's suggestedChildTasks field rather than making code changes; (5) set requestedStatus to 'done' when no gaps are found. The review template must contain no implementation instructions.

tasks.title: Register Run Review Agent command and wire role-aware single-pass stop behaviour
tasks.notes: Register 'Ralph: Run Review Agent' in src/commands/registerCommands.ts as a single-pass command with agentRole: 'review' and an agentId prefixed 'review-'. The review agent runs one iteration and stops — it does not loop. After the iteration completes, surface any proposed tasks (from suggestedChildTasks in the completion report) in the output channel and prompt the operator to run Apply Latest Task Decomposition Proposal if they want to commit them. Add the command to package.json contributes.commands. Do not add a Run Review Loop command — single-pass is intentional so the operator reviews proposals before a second pass.

tasks.title: Add regression coverage for review-agent prompt routing and command; document in docs/workflows.md
tasks.notes: Add tests in test/commandShell.smoke.test.ts: Run Review Agent command is registered; review-agent prompt selects the review template not the build template; single-pass stop fires after one iteration regardless of followUpAction. Add tests in test/promptBuilder.test.ts that the review template does not include implementation sections. Add a Review Agent subsection under Multi-Agent Team in docs/workflows.md describing: when to run a review pass, what the agent proposes vs implements, and the operator approval step before proposals become tasks. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Watchdog agent role — detects stuck or stale agents and drives claim recovery without per-claim operator intervention
tasks.notes: When a build agent gets stuck — stale claim, repeated no-progress, contested claim — the operator currently must intervene manually for each case. A watchdog agent runs independently, inspects claims.json and iterationHistory, identifies agents that have not made progress within a configurable TTL, and drives recovery: resolving stale claims, decomposing stuck tasks, or escalating to the operator with a structured report. The watchdog does not implement code — it manages the health of the agent team. It uses the same claims machinery but its reconciliation path permits cross-task recovery actions that are blocked for build agents.

tasks.title: Add watchdog agent prompt template that inspects claim health and emits structured recovery actions
tasks.notes: Add 'watchdog' to RalphAgentRole in src/ralph/types.ts (alongside T38.1's additions). Add a watchdog prompt template to prompt-templates/ instructing the model to: (1) read the mechanical health signals already surfaced by T36.4's checkStaleState preflight (stale locks, stale claims, stuck iterations) rather than re-deriving them from raw files; (2) read the recent iterationHistory for each flagged task and count trailing no-progress or repeated-failure iterations; (3) for each stuck agent propose one of: resolve_stale_claim (claim past TTL with no iteration activity), decompose_task (repeated no-progress with no file changes), or escalate_to_human (contested claim or repeated human-review-needed stop) with a severity tag (MEDIUM | HIGH | CRITICAL); (4) emit recovery decisions in the completion report as a structured watchdog_actions array with severity. The template must not propose code changes. The watchdog interprets mechanical signals — it does not detect them.

tasks.title: Wire watchdog recovery actions into reconciliation — permit cross-task resolve and decompose for watchdog role only
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/reconciliation.ts extend the completion report schema with an optional watchdog_actions array. When the reconciling agent's role is 'watchdog', process each action: resolve_stale_claim calls resolveStaleClaim for the named task and agent without requiring the watchdog to hold a claim on that task; decompose_task calls applySuggestedChildTasks for the named task after validating the proposal; escalate_to_human appends a structured entry to progress.md and sets the target task's blocker field. Non-watchdog agents that include watchdog_actions in their completion report must have the actions silently ignored — the role guard is authoritative.

tasks.title: Register Run Watchdog Agent command; add regression coverage and docs
tasks.notes: Register 'Ralph: Run Watchdog Agent' in src/commands/registerCommands.ts as a single-pass command with agentRole: 'watchdog' and agentId: 'watchdog'. Add tests: command is registered; watchdog_actions from a non-watchdog agent are silently ignored; watchdog can resolve a stale claim without holding a claim on the target task; watchdog decompose proposal is applied when valid. Add a Watchdog Agent subsection under Multi-Agent Team in docs/workflows.md describing: when to run the watchdog, what recovery actions it can take autonomously, and the escalation path when it cannot recover. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Source control agent role — structured commits, branch-per-task strategy, and PR creation on parent completion
tasks.notes: Currently Ralph makes no git commits — all file changes accumulate on the working branch until a human commits them. In a multi-agent team this creates merge risk: two agents can make overlapping changes that conflict silently until a human intervenes. A source control agent introduces structured commits (one per completed task), optional branch-per-task isolation, and automated PR creation when a parent task's children are all done. All git operations are disabled by default (scmStrategy: 'none') to preserve existing behaviour — operators opt in explicitly.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.scmStrategy setting and implement commit-on-done — auto-commit after each task is reconciled done
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.scmStrategy to package.json contributes.configuration as a string enum with values 'none' (default), 'commit-on-done', and 'branch-per-task'. In src/ralph/iterationEngine.ts after a task is reconciled done and the iteration result is persisted, if scmStrategy is 'commit-on-done': run git add -A (excluding .ralph/state.json, .ralph/claims.json, .ralph/logs/ — add these to .gitignore if absent), generate a commit message 'ralph(T<id>): <task title>\n\nAgent: <agentId> | Iteration: <n> | Validation: <status>', and run git commit. Record whether the commit succeeded in iteration result warnings. Add the scm strategy field to src/config/types.ts and src/config/readConfig.ts.

tasks.title: Implement branch-per-task SCM strategy — integration branch per parent, feature branch per child, atomic merge on parent done
tasks.notes: When scmStrategy is 'branch-per-task': for tasks with a parentId, create or reuse a ralph/integration/<parentId> branch from main (the accumulation branch for that parent's work); create a child feature branch ralph/<taskId> from the integration branch, not from main. Record both the integration branch and the base branch (the branch active at claim time) in the claim metadata. When a child task is reconciled done, commit remaining changes (same message format as commit-on-done with GIT_AUTHOR_NAME='ralph/<agentId>'), merge ralph/<taskId> into ralph/integration/<parentId> with a merge commit. When the parent task auto-completes (all children done), merge ralph/integration/<parentId> into the base branch with a single atomic merge commit referencing the parent task id and title — one integration merge to main per parent, not one per child. For top-level tasks with no parentId, use ralph/<taskId> directly off the base branch. If any merge fails due to conflicts: leave the conflicting branch checked out, reset the affected task to in_progress with a blocker noting the merge conflict, release the claim, and surface the conflict path in iteration result warnings. Never auto-delete ralph/integration/* or ralph/<taskId> branches.

tasks.title: Add ralphCodex.scmPrOnParentDone setting and implement PR creation via gh CLI on parent task completion
tasks.notes: Add ralphCodex.scmPrOnParentDone boolean setting (default false). When enabled and a parent task auto-completes with scmStrategy 'branch-per-task': push the ralph/integration/<parentId> branch to the remote and open a PR using 'gh pr create' with the parent task title and a body listing child task ids and their completion summaries, targeting the base branch recorded when the first child claim was acquired. If gh is unavailable or the push fails, surface the failure in iteration result warnings and continue — PR creation failure must not roll back task completion. Add regression coverage for the gh invocation and the failure-tolerant path. Add a Source Control Agent subsection under Multi-Agent Team in docs/workflows.md. Run npm run validate.

tasks.title: Add mechanical checkStaleState preflight check — in-process detection of stale locks, stale claims, and stuck iterations without LLM involvement
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/preflight.ts add a checkStaleState() function that runs in-process (no LLM, no external process) as part of every preflight check. It must detect: (1) state.lock file older than a configurable threshold (default 5 min) — surface as a warning with the lock file age and the instruction to remove it manually if no iteration is in progress; (2) tasks.lock file older than threshold — same warning pattern; (3) any active claim in claims.json whose claimedAt is past the stale TTL and has no matching iteration result in .ralph/artifacts/ from after the claim time — surface as a warning per claim with agentId, taskId, and age; (4) any active claim whose claimedAt is past TTL with no recent state.json lastRun timestamp — surface as 'agent may be offline'. These are mechanical signals only — checkStaleState does not take recovery actions. Recovery is the watchdog's job (T39.1). Ensure checkStaleState results appear in the preflight artifact and in Show Status under a dedicated Agent Health section.

tasks.title: Session handoff protocol — serialize mid-task context on loop termination so the next session resumes without re-reading full iteration history
tasks.notes: Ralph loops terminate cleanly for many reasons that are not failures: context window exhaustion, control_plane_reload_required, manual stop, iteration cap. Currently the next session re-reads the full iteration history to reconstruct context. Gastown's gt handoff model solves this: on clean termination, serialize a structured handoff note capturing what was done and what remains, so the next session picks up precisely where the previous one stopped — without re-deriving context from raw artifacts. This is the complement to T32 (auto-reload): T32 handles the trigger, T41 handles the context carry-over.

tasks.title: Write a structured handoff note to .ralph/handoff/ on every clean loop termination
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/iterationEngine.ts, after persisting the final iteration result on any clean stop (task_marked_complete, iteration_cap_reached, control_plane_reload_required, human_review_needed, no_actionable_task, verification_passed_no_remaining_subtasks), write a handoff file to .ralph/handoff/<agentId>-<iteration>.json containing: agentId, iteration, selectedTaskId, selectedTaskTitle, stopReason, completionClassification, progressNote (from the completion report), pendingBlocker (if any), validationFailureSignature (if any), and a brief humanSummary string. The file must be written atomically (write to .tmp then rename). Add .ralph/handoff/ to the generated-artifact retention policy so old handoff files are pruned alongside prompts and run artifacts. The handoff directory must not be committed — add it to .gitignore.

tasks.title: Include the latest handoff note in the next iteration's prompt context and preflight output
tasks.notes: In src/ralph/iterationPreparation.ts, before building the prompt context, check for a handoff file matching the current agentId in .ralph/handoff/. If one exists for the immediately preceding iteration (iteration - 1), include its humanSummary, pendingBlocker, and validationFailureSignature in the prompt's Prior Context section under a 'Session Handoff' heading — placed above the general prior-iteration verifier context so it is the first context the model reads. In src/ralph/preflight.ts, surface the existence of an active handoff note as an informational diagnostic so operators can see that the session is resuming from a handoff. Add regression coverage in test/iterationEngine.integration.test.ts proving: a handoff file is written on clean termination; the next iteration includes handoff context in the rendered prompt; handoff files are pruned by generated-artifact retention. Run npm run validate.
